text_content: INTERNATIONAL LASER
DISPLAY ASSOCIATION
7062 Edgeworth DriveOrlando, FL 32819(407) 797-7654mail@ilda.comwww.ilda.com
ILDA COMMENTS ON DRAFT RULE OF 25 TAC §289.301
Registration and Radiation Safety Requirements for Lasers and Intense- Pulsed Light Devices
My name is Patrick Murphy. I am the Executive Director of the International Laser Display Association, a not-for-profit trade association formed in 1986.
ILDA members create and perform laser light shows, as well as providing hardware, software and artwork (stock frames and shows) for laser light shows.
Roughly half of our current 200+ members are U.S. based. A few of our overseas members also tour with shows into the United States.
As far as Texas goes, we have 10 current members in Texas, as well as 21 former members with a Texas address.
In 2012, ILDA held our Annual Conference in San Antonio, Texas.
Texas regulations affect not only our members who live and work in the state, but also many U.S. members who bring tours into Texas and even international Members also touring in the state.
OVERVIEW OF THESE COMMENTS
The major points of ILDA’s comments are as follows:

1) The comment period of two weeks is far too short. Especially since the comment deadline is July 5. Many or most laser light show companies perform shows over the July 4 holiday. It is not just the Fourth itself, it is also many days prior, when traveling and setting up the show. Please extend the comment period to at least July 12 or even July 19.
Page 1 of 14

Worldwide, over the past 50 years there have been only about 5 documented eye injuries to the general public from the type of lasers (continuous wave) used in today’s laser light shows. This low injury incidence — even including widespread unregulated or illegal audience scanning in the U.S. and overseas — indicates that strict monitoring and detailed regulatory oversight is not a necessary condition for public safety.

There are only five U.S. states that have registration requirements either for laser light show lasers, or for the show itself equipment. The other 45 states have no statewide requirements. (There are also five localities with registration requirements.)

Texas has not demonstrated any injuries to the general public or laser light show technicians, or other actual reasons to regulate laser light shows in a similar manner to medical, industrial, academic, or research uses which have had documented injuries.

Texas should piggyback off the U.S. Food and Drug Administration’s existing procedures. Simply require laser light show operators to have valid variances, annual reports and other documents required by FDA. These are already required by FDA to be onsite at every laser light show. Texas could also require copies of these to be emailed or sent via postal mail to DSHS upon request.

The requirement for out-of-state laser light show operators to have a state registration agent should be dropped. No other state radiological health agency has a requirement for an in-state agent to submit forms or receive communications from laser light show operators. FDA similarly deals directly with the laser light show operator as they submit variances, annual reports and other required forms.

Texas should consider dropping the requirement for advance notification of shows. FDA used to require this but they no longer do. One ILDA Member told me that “many years ago” Texas used to inspect their shows but this is not the case now. If shows are safe — no injuries over 50 years — and inspections are not done, then there is no justification for requiring advance notification.

However, if Texas does keep the requirement for advance notification, the required information should be pared to the basics. Because the applicant must have an FDA variance, and other FDA-required documentation (including an FAA letter of determination for outdoor unterminated shows) Texas’ form should not ask for information that is already in the FDA- and FAA-required forms.

Texas still can control laser light show safety. Any laser light show which does not provide advance notification (if this requirement is kept) and/or which does not have valid FDA variances and other necessary FDA documents, can be shut down. This could be done by DSHS notifying the person paying for the show and/or the venue operator (as per the contact info in the 7-day advance notification). It could also be done by DSHS asking local police or fire department to inspect the show for the necessary documentation and shutting it down if this does not exist.
Page 2 of 14
It should not be a requirement that a Laser Safety Officer be tied or registered to a particular laser device or projector. This is not done anywhere else, to the best of my knowledge.

It should not be a requirement that a Laser Safety Officer operate a laser light show, or be in attendance at a laser light show. However, a designated Laser Safety Officer for the laser light show must train and be responsible for the Laser Operator who actually operates the laser light show. (Of course, a LSO can operate the show as well if he or she wishes.)

All of the above comments are for laser light shows using continuous wave lasers, and with the laser powers most commonly used today. If Texas wants to require registration of light show lasers, this should be limited to lasers which have caused actual harm or which are significantly stronger than those used for the past 50 years. Specifically, Texas should only require registration of pulsed lasers used in laser light shows, and of lasers emitting one or more beams over an average power of a certain limit (provisionally, 50 watts though this is subject to change after I get more input from safety experts and show operators).

The regulations repeat some information from sources such as ANSI, IEC and FDA. If this information is deemed necessary to include, make explicit where the source is, and especially make explicit if there are any changes or differences from ANSI, IEC and FDA.
The remainder of this document goes into more detail on the above comments. At the end of the document is some suggested specific language and suggestions.
ILDA LASER SHOW SAFETY CONTRIBUTIONS
ILDA has been a strong advocate for laser light show safety since its founding.

ILDA is a voting member on committees which write the ANSI Z136.1 “Safe Use of Lasers” standard, the Z136.6 “Safe Use of Lasers Outdoors” standard, and the ANSI Z136.10 “Safe Use of Lasers in Entertainment, Displays and Exhibitions” draft standard.

ILDA is also a voting member of the SAE G10T “Laser Safety Hazards” committee which develops recommended practices for aviators, FAA, FDA and others involved with lasers in airspace. I personally am co-chair of the SAE G10-OL “Operational Lasers” committee which focuses on how pilots can be trained and protected from outdoor laser hazards. Last year, I also personally assisted FAA staff in updating forms and instructions for submitting outdoor laser uses (of all types, not just light shows) for safety evaluation by FAA.

ILDA provides four laser safety courses: Laser Safety Officer (LSO), Lasershow Operator, Audience Scanning Safety, and Lasershow Safety Inspector. Since 2010, there have been 346 persons taking our 8.5 hour LSO course and passing the 2-hour test. In addition, ILDA Board Member Roberta McHatton teaches her own, separate, course which as of July 2023 has had 404 persons she has certified as Laser Safety Officers.
Page 3 of 14
In conclusion, ILDA has a decades-long commitment to making laser shows safe for audiences, technicians and the general public.
MOST STATES HAVE NO REGISTRATION REQUIREMENT

To the best of ILDA’s knowledge, there are five U.S. states with laser light show-related registration and/or notification requirements: Arizona, Florida, New York, Massachusetts, and Texas. In Illinois registration is currently suggested but not required.
This means 90% of the states have no laser light show-related registration/notification requirements.
In addition, there are five localities in the U.S. which require permits for laser light shows: Orange County (Florida), Orlando, Cleveland, Palm Springs and Portland.
Again, the vast majority of localities do not require permits for laser light shows.

As discussed below, there have been only five documented eye injuries, worldwide, in the past 50 years. Thus, the safety record of all the states and localities (and countries) without laser light show specific restrictions is excellent.
Let us now turn to the Texas call for comments and the specific proposal. EXTEND COMMENT PERIOD

The comment period for this is only two weeks, June 21 to July 5 2023. This is just 8 working days not counting Monday July 3, which many companies take off, and Tuesday July 4 which is a Federal Holiday.
The comment period includes the Fourth of July, which is always a very busy one for laser light show operators, for obvious reasons.

One prominent Texas-based laser light show company wrote to me, “I’m on a show trip deep in West Texas, this is a complete surprise! ... Timing could not be worse.” This person said his laser show tour would be complete on July 17, at which time he returns to his office.

In contrast to Texas, federal government agencies have 30, 60 or 90 day comment periods in the Federal Register; none as short as 14 days. Also, on July 1 Health Canada issued proposed laser regulations with a 75-day comment period until September 14.
Two weeks — especially over the July 4 holiday — is much too short.

If Texas truly wants meaningful comments from a variety of Texas citizens and others affected by the proposed regulations, ILDA requests that the comment period be extended at least one week, to July 12 or even better to July 19.
Page 4 of 14
This would give additional time for laser light show operators to be finished with their July 4 shows, so they can provide thoughtful comments.
NO JUSTIFICATION GIVEN

ILDA has not been able to find any justification for the original Texas radiation control requirements imposed on laser light show users. Nor does ILDA see any additional justification for the latest changes.
Specifically, ILDA is unaware of any documented injuries to either the general public, or to technicians, arising from laser light shows in Texas.
AN EXCELLENT SAFETY RECORD FOR CW LASERS

Since 1996 ILDA has been actively looking for reports of laser light show injuries to the general public. To the best of our knowledge, there have been roughly five such injuries, worldwide, from continuous wave lasers since laser shows began about 50 years ago. This is about one per decade, worldwide.

This tally counts not only U.S. regulated laser light shows, but also overseas shows. The majority of these shows are not done to U.S.-type regulations. They usually involve directly scanning laser light onto the audience (which FDA prohibits unless a special “audience scanning variance” has been approved).

Even in the U.S., many or perhaps a majority of laser light shows are done illegally, with no FDA variance. Almost all laser light shows are never inspected by FDA. Both of these facts are of concern to ILDA.

From a safety standpoint, however, these facts indicate that even without much regulatory oversight, laser light show eye injuries from CW lasers are exceedingly unlikely — on the order of one per decade of laser light shows, worldwide.

In 2009, ILDA presented a 31-page paper at the International Laser Safety Conference regarding this topic in much more detail. The paper includes reasons why laser light is much more hazardous close to the laser (e.g, for technicians, persons in industry and medicine, etc.) than it is for an audience further away. The paper is online at ilda.com/resources/Safety/ audience-scanning_overview_latest.pdf. The first 17 pages are most relevant to this discussion. We ask them to be included in our comments by reference.
PULSED LASERS REQUIRE REGULATION

The above discussion of safety is true for continuous wave lasers. Unfortunately, there have been about 50 injuries from pulsed lasers over the same time period. The good news is that almost all laser shows today use only continuous wave lasers. We believe any potential issue
Page 5 of 14
with pulsed lasers in Texas can be handled under the current and proposed regulations. This will be discussed later.
NO REQUIREMENT FOR A REGULATORY BURDEN

ILDA assumes that Texas’ policy is to involve government when necessary, but also to reduce the regulatory burden on it’s citizens and businesses when this is justified. We also assume Texas is a “pro-business” state that wants to see its businesses succeed and not be tied up in unnecessary red tape.

ILDA’s recommendation therefore is that Texas drop both the requirement for registration of continuous wave light show lasers, and related requirements such as notification of Texas laser light shows.
Again, if there has been no harm to Texans (or other persons worldwide), why are light show lasers and their use regulated to such a degree?
FUNDING THE DEPARTMENT?

ILDA does agree that persons and companies being regulated should help pay for necessary regulations and oversight (e.g., inspectors, paperwork review, etc.). However, this assumes that the regulations are necessary.
If registration of light show lasers is being done primarily to help fund the Department of State Health Services, we believe this is wrong.
As stated above, ILDA does not see any assertions or evidence from DSHS of any injury to Texans from laser light shows.
PIGGYBACK OFF U.S. FDA VARIANCES

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration requires that a variance be obtained for any laser light show or display of 5 milliwatts or more which is entered into commerce. An FDA variance covers both the laser light show equipment and how it is used in a laser light show.

There are four relevant FDA Forms: 3632 for the laser projector, 3147 for the variance, 3640 for the laser light show itself, and 3636 for the annual report due every year. These reports (or links to the projector reports) are required by FDA to be onsite at every laser light show and display.

Thus, if Texas wants to review these, they are onsite at a laser light show. If they are not onsite, the show is illegal from a federal standpoint and Texas certainly would be within its rights to ban or stop the show.
Texas could also require that copies be emailed or postal mailed to, DSHS upon request, based on a reasonable rationale for the request.
Page 6 of 14

Having these documents onsite at a laser light show, or providing them upon request to DSHS, helps prove that the laser light show producer has followed FDA regulations. This turn implies they will be doing a safe and legal show.

If the forms are not available onsite, then the laser light show should not be permitted. Similarly, if copies of the requested forms are not emailed or sent via postal mail to DSHS, the person or company should not be permitted to perform laser light shows in Texas.
ADVANCE NOTIFICATION SHOULD NO LONGER BE REQUIRED

FDA has had so few problems with laser light shows that they no longer variance holders to provide, in advance, a list of laser light show tour itineraries. They can request an itinerary of any variance holder, but as far as ILDA knows, FDA does not often do this.

Since FDA does not require such notification, ILDA believes that advance notification of laser light shows should also not be required by Texas, especially in light of the lack of documented injuries from laser light shows in Texas.
IF ADVANCE NOTIFICATION CONTINUES TO BE REQUIRED

If Texas continues to require advance notification about laser light shows, at the very least the information requested should be scaled back substantially. Most of the information requested on the current BRC Form 301-C is already in the FDA documentation. There is no reason for Texas to require the applicant to repeat information already in the FDA-approved variance and other forms.

Now, if a person or company filling out the advance notification form does NOT have FDA documentation, then DSHS should be required to refuse permission for the show, and should take positive steps to ensure that the show not be performed.

This is not so much for safety reasons, since as stated above there appear to be no injuries from laser light show operations in Texas. The reason is for regulatory fairness. It is unfair and possibly illegal if Texas actively requires laser light show operators to have relevant FDA and FAA approvals, but then does not stop shows without such approvals.

ILDA would also ask that DSHS take positive steps to ensure that the show not be performed. This could include contacting the person who hired the laser show and/or the venue owner, to tell them the show cannot proceed unless proper FDA documentation is provided in time to DSHS. (This means the form should ask for the name and contact information of the person or company hiring the laser light show, and of the venue representative if known.) This could also include having the police or fire department, or other authority check the venue on the show date to ensure lasers are not being used, and are stopped if they are in use.
Page 7 of 14

Note that if DSHS decides not to require advance notification, as recommended by ILDA, then there would be no legal requirement for DSHS to actively monitor and stop shows that do not have FDA approval.
NO NEED FOR STATE REGISTRATION AGENT
ILDA is concerned about the requirement for a state registration agent, for show producers located outside of Texas.
We are unaware of any state radiological health agency that requires such registration. (New York State has a somewhat different system, run through their Department of Labor, not radiation safety.)

Requiring a state agent to file RC Form 301-05 (BRC Form 301-3) is unnecessary. Certainly if someone has obtained a valid laser light show variance from FDA, they are qualified at the federal level at least. FDA did not require them to go through an agent.
TEXAS ADOPTION OF FDA VARIANCES

FDA allows intentional scanning of audiences with laser light if the variance applicant is using special equipment and procedures. Such an “audience scanning variance” will include an attachment specifically addressing such equipment and procedures.
ILDA is concerned about language such as paragraph (c)(2) which prohibits individuals being intentionally exposed to laser and IPL radiation above the MPE.

This could be interpreted by an overzealous DSHS official as prohibiting any audience exposure, even FDA-approved audience scanning. As far as we know, FDA does not allow audience scanning above the MPE. But this distinction may be lost on an official with insufficient knowledge or training.

Therefore, ILDA suggests having language in the Texas regulations similar to this: “Laser light shows performed in Texas must be done in accordance with the requirements stated in the FDA variance and any other relevant FDA forms, documents and regulations.”
LASER SAFETY OFFICERS ARE SEPARATE FROM LASERS

ILDA and Board Member Roberta McHatton have trained 750 Laser Safety Officers specifically for doing laser light shows. These persons have passed tests in order to receive a Certificate of Completion. Both ILDA and Roberta are on the ANSI Z136.1 “Safe Use of Lasers” committee which developed and updates definitions including that of Laser Safety Officers.
We therefore have some expertise in the definition and requirements for a Laser Safety Officer.
Page 8 of 14

In the Definitions section, the first part of the definition of “Laser Safety Officer” is fine. (“An individual who has a knowledge of and the authority and responsibility to apply appropriate laser radiation protection rules, standards, and practices.”) This accords with ANSI and other states’ definitions.

However, ILDA strongly recommends that Texas’ definition of “Laser Safety Officer” NOT include the phrase of “and who must be specifically authorized on a certificate of laser registration.” This a Texas-specific requirement which would not apply in other states.
Furthermore, regardless of the definition wording, LSOs should not be coupled to laser registration certificates — at least not for laser light shows. This is for three reasons:
The lack of injuries from laser light shows means there should be little or no regulation beyond existing, sufficient federal regulations.

An LSO is not responsible under ANSI or other states for a specific laser. They are responsible for all lasers being used under their supervision — but not for a specific model or serial number laser.
This would be like requiring all drivers’ licenses to be specifically tied to a certificate of automobile registration. I would not be able to drive a car other than the one(s) registered in my name.
LSOs ARE DIFFERENT FROM LASER OPERATORS
ILDA is very concerned that Texas either does not understand a laser light show LSO’s responsibilities, or that the responsibilities are unclear in the regulations.
A laser light show Laser Safety Officer is responsible for the safety of the show, both in design and operation. However, the LSO does NOT have to be the person who operates the show.

This is a separate safety position, the Laser Operator. (ILDA uses the slightly different wording “Lasershow Operator” to make clear that this person is operating the show as well as the laser device).

On page 20 is a requirement that the LSO “assume control and have the authority to institute corrective actions to include the shutdown of operations when necessary in emergencies or unsafe conditions.” This incorrectly implies that the LSO is the one who is assuming control or is otherwise in direct control of operations.
A better wording would be something like the following:

“If the LSO is not in direct control of the laser operation, they shall be responsible for the training and actions of a Laser Operator. The Laser Operator shall be trained on the operation of the laser, laser system, and associated laser safety devices and features. The training shall cover both normal operation, and what to do in case of abnormal operation,
Page 9 of 14
misalignment, malfunctions, and other reasonably foreseeable potential hazards. The Laser Operator shall follow all safety instructions of the Laser Safety Officer.”
That is what actually happens at most laser light shows. It is not necessary for the Laser Operator to have all of the knowledge that an LSO must have.

In many cases, the Laser Operator’s duties are basic: To activate the laser at the appropriate time, to monitor the operation, and — if safety hazards arise or are imminent — to shut off the laser or take other corrective action.
LASER LIGHT SHOWS NEEDING REGULATION
As stated earlier, the lack of injuries from laser light shows implies that laser registration and advance notification is not needed at this time.
There may be two special types of lasers where registration and notification are justified:

Lasers that operate, or could be operated, in pulsed mode. This means the laser light is emitted in pulses rather than continuously. This could be a single pulse, or more commonly in laser light shows, in repetitive pulses. Note that “pulsed laser” does NOT include lasers whose light is emitted continuously (“continuous wave” or “CW”) and is then modulated or chopped to create a pulsed effect.
The reason for this is that pulsed lasers in general have a much higher peak power in each pulse. Pulsed lasers misused have caused injuries at a number of laser shows in the past 50 years.

The worst documented laser light show injury incident was in July 2008 when a pulsed laser was aimed at an audience during a rave in Russia. Thirty-five persons went to the hospital with eye injuries or complaints. Four were found to have permanent injuries (though no permanent blindness as far as ILDA knows).

In our Laser Safety Officer classes, both ILDA and Roberta McHatton stress that pulsed lasers should never be used on or near any person. Fortunately, almost all laser shows today use CW lasers exclusively.

Lasers that are over a certain average power, whether pulsed or CW. ILDA’s preliminary suggestion is that Texas register laser light show lasers emitting one or more beams with 50 or more watts average power. Note that this power level is preliminary, because ILDA has not yet consulted with other laser light show safety experts, and those doing laser light shows, to see if they concur with this particular wattage.

It is also important to note the difference between “50 watts per beam” and a 50 watt laser. Some laser projection devices use a diffraction grating to break up a single beam into multiple, simultaneous “beamlets”. Each beamlet will have a fraction of the original laser
Page 10 of 14
power. So any power limit should be per beam (e.g., the highest power of any beam possible from the laser projector), not the power of the laser inside the projector.

This is NOT the case for scanned beams, where a single beam is rapidly moved by tiny galvanometer mirrors under computer control. Although the average power of the beam is reduced by scanning, it is possible for scanning to stop due to mechanical failure (a broken scanner) or, more likely, due to improper software control. Laser show projectors using scanners should be evaluated for the maximum output power of the laser, regardless of whether the beam is scanned or is stationary.

In general, if Texas wants to register laser light show lasers, this should only be done for hazardous lasers such as pulsed lasers, or those with powers that are significantly above the powers that have been used for the past 50 years.

Thus far ILDA has not seen injuries from higher-powered lasers. But since safe and effective shows have been done for 50 years with powers less than 50 watts per beam, ILDA does not see any demonstrated need for registering light show lasers less than 50 watts (or whatever wattage is recommended as “extra powerful” by laser light show safety experts and those doing shows).
COST OF REGISTRATION

It is not clear from RC 204 (Fee Schedule) if the registration cost for “Laser - Industrial/ Services/Entertainment” is $400 per laser or $400 per registering all lasers being used. (Hopefully it is $400 per person or company that is registering their lasers.)

Either way, ILDA believes that registration should not be required for CW laser light show lasers under a power limit (provisionally 50 watt output) due to the demonstrated lack of injuries both in Texas and worldwide.
There may be justification for registering pulsed lasers used in entertainment, or lasers emitting one or more beams over a certain wattage.
REPEATING INFORMATION IN OTHER DOCUMENTS
In the regulation, there is a large amount of information that repeats what is in ANSI Z136 “Safe Use of Lasers”, IEC 60825-1 “Safety of laser products”, and U.S. FDA regulations.
The repeated material includes definitions, practices and signage requirements.
It would be very helpful to specifically state both when Texas’ regulations are the same as these sources, and when the regs differ.
Page 11 of 14

For those who are already familiar with ANSI, IEC and FDA, this lets us “skip over” parts that are the same. More importantly, it highlights or warns us of any areas where Texas’ requirements differ from ANSI, IEC and FDA.
SPECIFIC LANGUAGE
Here is a list of the most important sections we recommend to be removed or changed:
(d)(33), definition of Laser Safety Officer: Remove the second phrase “and who must be specifically authorized on a certificate of laser registration.”
(f)(1)(E): We believe registration of laser light show lasers should not be required, due to the lack of demonstrated injuries caused by such lasers.

(g)(1)(C) and subsections (i) and (ii): If registration of light show lasers is deemed to be required, we believe such registration of laser light show lasers should not need to be accompanied by a Business Information Form or the other requirements in (i) and (ii). It should be sufficient for the applicant to list their business name on the application form, and if necessary, to also list their business registration number as issued by Texas or their state (or province for non-U.S. businesses). ILDA does not know why Texas goes to lengths that even FDA does not to “prove” that a business is actually a business.

(g)(5) Application for laser light show: As stated earlier, we do not see any demonstrated harm from laser light shows nor any demonstrated necessity to register shows (as opposed to registering light show laser devices). We therefore recommend that section (g)(5) be rewritten using language such as this:
(g)(5) “Laser light shows

(A) Any laser light shows using pulsed lasers, or using high-power lasers (where one or more emitted beams is over 50 watts), shall apply for and receive a certificate of laser registration for a pulsed or high-power laser light show before beginning any such show.

Any application for a pulsed or high-power laser light show must be signed by the applicant, registrant, or a person duly authorized to act for and on behalf of the applicant or registrant. The application must also be signed by the LSO, if the applicant or registrant is not a Laser Safety Officer.
All pulsed lasers, and lasers where one or more emitted beams is over 50 watts shall be registered with the state.

(B) All laser light shows performed in the state of Texas shall have a valid variance issued from the FDA, along with evidence of annual report(s) keeping the variance valid, and all other documents required by FDA. For shows where laser light enters or traverses navigable airspace (including potential navigation by helicopters and emergency aircraft),
Page 12 of 14
the show shall also have a valid Letter of Determination from the Federal Aviation Administration, not objecting to the show and its effects.
All documentation required by FDA (and, if appropriate, FAA) shall be available onsite for inspection upon demand.

The department may contact the laser light show LSO, producer, or other official to obtain a copy of any FDA or FAA document required by FDA to be onsite during a laser light show. There shall be stated reasons or justification for the demand.

If any laser light show performed or planned to be performed in the state of Texas does not have all valid documents required by FDA (including, if appropriate, an FAA Letter of Determination not objecting to the show), then the show cannot proceed.”
This also implies a new definition in section (d):

(d)(23) High-powered laser—For purposes of these regulations, a laser where one or more of the emitted beams has a power greater than 50 watts. If diffraction gratings or other elements are used to modify a laser, normally emitting over 50 watts, so no single beam is over 50 watts, then the grating or other element shall be fixed such that it cannot be removed during operation without the use of tools.
Continuing the list of recommended changes:

(p)(2)(A) [new subsection]: “If the LSO is not in direct control of the laser operation, they shall be responsible for the training and actions of a Laser Operator. The Laser Operator shall be trained on the operation of the laser, laser system, and associated laser safety devices and features. The training shall cover both normal operation, and what to do in case of abnormal operation, misalignment, malfunctions, and other reasonably foreseeable potential hazards. The Laser Operator shall follow all safety instructions of the Laser Safety Officer.”

(q)(1)(A) [new subsection]: “For laser light shows, exposure of any individual to laser light on their body (including on clothing) shall not be permitted except as authorized in a valid FDA variance which specifically allows such exposure.”

(q)(3)(A) through (q)(4) — Much of this parallels requirements already in 21 CFR 1040.10 and/or 1040.11, and other FDA documents such as a laser product report and variances. The Texas requirements should be reviewed to remove any duplicated requirements.

(s)(1) — This section does not define “access” to IPL, Class 3B or Class 4 levels of laser radiation. Often in laser light shows a person is mounting a projector while it is off (no access) and then is far from the laser while aligning, rehearsing and performing the laser light show. In such cases, protective eyewear is not needed, just as it is not needed for the audience. (In fact, it would be counterproductive for an audience to wear protective eyewear as they cannot see the laser effects.) This provision should be clarified for laser light show personnel.
Page 13 of 14

(u)(3)(D) through (u)(3)(G) — We believe that FDA allows use of the term “light” instead of “laser radiation” or “electromagnetic radiation” or similar, for laser radiation in the 400 -700 nanometer wavelength range. Therefore we recommend a new subsection:

(u)(3)(G)(iii): “The word ‘light’ may be used in place of ‘laser radiation’ or ‘electromagnetic radiation’ on labels and signs required by this subparagraph for wavelengths that are exclusively in the range of 400 to 700 nm.”

(u)(4): This language should be moved higher in the (u) section, perhaps even to the start. The reason is that the knowledgable reader will not have to wade through all of section (u) to find out that their existing FDA, ANSI or IEC labels and signage are acceptable by the state of Texas. They should be told this “upfront” so they can skim or skip the rest of section (u). (If there are any Texas-specific labels or signage, not required by FDA, ANSI or IEC, these should be called out for the knowledgeable reader.)

(x): The phrase “calibrated and designed” should be clarified and strengthened to “calibrated, designed, and appropriate”. Why? To give an example, silicon power meters are appropriate for measuring moving beams; they can give an instant indication of the power level. However, thermal meters — which can measure static beams without a problem — are not appropriate for measuring moving beams. This clarifies that the meter must be appropriate for the use of the laser being tested.
SUMMARY
ILDA greatly appreciates that Texas is concerned with laser safety in general, and with laser light shows in particular.
However, the association believes there is some overregulation for laser light shows. ILDA hopes that Texas will reexamine their requirements with a fresh eye.
Sincerely,
Patrick Murphy
Executive Director, International Laser Display Association
On behalf of ILDA’s Board of Directors:
Brian Gonzalez, President
Roberta McHatton
Theo Petrides
Horacio Pugliese
Christopher Short
Page 14 of 14

INTERNATIONAL LASER
DISPLAY ASSOCIATION
7062 Edgeworth DriveOrlando, FL 32819(407) 797-7654mail@ilda.comwww.ilda.com

ILDA COMMENTS ON DRAFT RULE OF 25 TAC §289.301
Registration and Radiation Safety Requirements for Lasers and Intense- Pulsed Light Devices
My name is Patrick Murphy. I am the Executive Director of the International Laser Display Association, a not-for-profit trade association formed in 1986.
ILDA members create and perform laser light shows, as well as providing hardware, software and artwork (stock frames and shows) for laser light shows.
Roughly half of our current 200+ members are U.S. based. A few of our overseas members also tour with shows into the United States.
As far as Texas goes, we have 10 current members in Texas, as well as 21 former members with a Texas address.
In 2012, ILDA held our Annual Conference in San Antonio, Texas.
Texas regulations affect not only our members who live and work in the state, but also many U.S. members who bring tours into Texas and even international Members also touring in the state.
OVERVIEW OF THESE COMMENTS
The major points of ILDA’s comments are as follows:
1) The comment period of two weeks is far too short. Especially since the comment deadline is July 5. Many or most laser light show companies perform shows over the July 4 holiday. It is not just the Fourth itself, it is also many days prior, when traveling and setting up the show. Please extend the comment period to at least July 12 or even July 19.
Page 1 of 14
Worldwide, over the past 50 years there have been only about 5 documented eye injuries to the general public from the type of lasers (continuous wave) used in today’s laser light shows. This low injury incidence — even including widespread unregulated or illegal audience scanning in the U.S. and overseas — indicates that strict monitoring and detailed regulatory oversight is not a necessary condition for public safety.
There are only five U.S. states that have registration requirements either for laser light show lasers, or for the show itself equipment. The other 45 states have no statewide requirements. (There are also five localities with registration requirements.)
Texas has not demonstrated any injuries to the general public or laser light show technicians, or other actual reasons to regulate laser light shows in a similar manner to medical, industrial, academic, or research uses which have had documented injuries.
Texas should piggyback off the U.S. Food and Drug Administration’s existing procedures. Simply require laser light show operators to have valid variances, annual reports and other documents required by FDA. These are already required by FDA to be onsite at every laser light show. Texas could also require copies of these to be emailed or sent via postal mail to DSHS upon request.
The requirement for out-of-state laser light show operators to have a state registration agent should be dropped. No other state radiological health agency has a requirement for an in-state agent to submit forms or receive communications from laser light show operators. FDA similarly deals directly with the laser light show operator as they submit variances, annual reports and other required forms.
Texas should consider dropping the requirement for advance notification of shows. FDA used to require this but they no longer do. One ILDA Member told me that “many years ago” Texas used to inspect their shows but this is not the case now. If shows are safe — no injuries over 50 years — and inspections are not done, then there is no justification for requiring advance notification.
However, if Texas does keep the requirement for advance notification, the required information should be pared to the basics. Because the applicant must have an FDA variance, and other FDA-required documentation (including an FAA letter of determination for outdoor unterminated shows) Texas’ form should not ask for information that is already in the FDA- and FAA-required forms.
Texas still can control laser light show safety. Any laser light show which does not provide advance notification (if this requirement is kept) and/or which does not have valid FDA variances and other necessary FDA documents, can be shut down. This could be done by DSHS notifying the person paying for the show and/or the venue operator (as per the contact info in the 7-day advance notification). It could also be done by DSHS asking local police or fire department to inspect the show for the necessary documentation and shutting it down if this does not exist.
Page 2 of 14
It should not be a requirement that a Laser Safety Officer be tied or registered to a particular laser device or projector. This is not done anywhere else, to the best of my knowledge.
It should not be a requirement that a Laser Safety Officer operate a laser light show, or be in attendance at a laser light show. However, a designated Laser Safety Officer for the laser light show must train and be responsible for the Laser Operator who actually operates the laser light show. (Of course, a LSO can operate the show as well if he or she wishes.)
All of the above comments are for laser light shows using continuous wave lasers, and with the laser powers most commonly used today. If Texas wants to require registration of light show lasers, this should be limited to lasers which have caused actual harm or which are significantly stronger than those used for the past 50 years. Specifically, Texas should only require registration of pulsed lasers used in laser light shows, and of lasers emitting one or more beams over an average power of a certain limit (provisionally, 50 watts though this is subject to change after I get more input from safety experts and show operators).
The regulations repeat some information from sources such as ANSI, IEC and FDA. If this information is deemed necessary to include, make explicit where the source is, and especially make explicit if there are any changes or differences from ANSI, IEC and FDA.
The remainder of this document goes into more detail on the above comments. At the end of the document is some suggested specific language and suggestions.
ILDA LASER SHOW SAFETY CONTRIBUTIONS
ILDA has been a strong advocate for laser light show safety since its founding.
ILDA is a voting member on committees which write the ANSI Z136.1 “Safe Use of Lasers” standard, the Z136.6 “Safe Use of Lasers Outdoors” standard, and the ANSI Z136.10 “Safe Use of Lasers in Entertainment, Displays and Exhibitions” draft standard.
ILDA is also a voting member of the SAE G10T “Laser Safety Hazards” committee which develops recommended practices for aviators, FAA, FDA and others involved with lasers in airspace. I personally am co-chair of the SAE G10-OL “Operational Lasers” committee which focuses on how pilots can be trained and protected from outdoor laser hazards. Last year, I also personally assisted FAA staff in updating forms and instructions for submitting outdoor laser uses (of all types, not just light shows) for safety evaluation by FAA.
ILDA provides four laser safety courses: Laser Safety Officer (LSO), Lasershow Operator, Audience Scanning Safety, and Lasershow Safety Inspector. Since 2010, there have been 346 persons taking our 8.5 hour LSO course and passing the 2-hour test. In addition, ILDA Board Member Roberta McHatton teaches her own, separate, course which as of July 2023 has had 404 persons she has certified as Laser Safety Officers.
Page 3 of 14
In conclusion, ILDA has a decades-long commitment to making laser shows safe for audiences, technicians and the general public.
MOST STATES HAVE NO REGISTRATION REQUIREMENT
To the best of ILDA’s knowledge, there are five U.S. states with laser light show-related registration and/or notification requirements: Arizona, Florida, New York, Massachusetts, and Texas. In Illinois registration is currently suggested but not required.
This means 90% of the states have no laser light show-related registration/notification requirements.
In addition, there are five localities in the U.S. which require permits for laser light shows: Orange County (Florida), Orlando, Cleveland, Palm Springs and Portland.
Again, the vast majority of localities do not require permits for laser light shows.
As discussed below, there have been only five documented eye injuries, worldwide, in the past 50 years. Thus, the safety record of all the states and localities (and countries) without laser light show specific restrictions is excellent.
Let us now turn to the Texas call for comments and the specific proposal. EXTEND COMMENT PERIOD
The comment period for this is only two weeks, June 21 to July 5 2023. This is just 8 working days not counting Monday July 3, which many companies take off, and Tuesday July 4 which is a Federal Holiday.
The comment period includes the Fourth of July, which is always a very busy one for laser light show operators, for obvious reasons.
One prominent Texas-based laser light show company wrote to me, “I’m on a show trip deep in West Texas, this is a complete surprise! ... Timing could not be worse.” This person said his laser show tour would be complete on July 17, at which time he returns to his office.
In contrast to Texas, federal government agencies have 30, 60 or 90 day comment periods in the Federal Register; none as short as 14 days. Also, on July 1 Health Canada issued proposed laser regulations with a 75-day comment period until September 14.
Two weeks — especially over the July 4 holiday — is much too short.
If Texas truly wants meaningful comments from a variety of Texas citizens and others affected by the proposed regulations, ILDA requests that the comment period be extended at least one week, to July 12 or even better to July 19.
Page 4 of 14
This would give additional time for laser light show operators to be finished with their July 4 shows, so they can provide thoughtful comments.
NO JUSTIFICATION GIVEN
ILDA has not been able to find any justification for the original Texas radiation control requirements imposed on laser light show users. Nor does ILDA see any additional justification for the latest changes.
Specifically, ILDA is unaware of any documented injuries to either the general public, or to technicians, arising from laser light shows in Texas.
AN EXCELLENT SAFETY RECORD FOR CW LASERS
Since 1996 ILDA has been actively looking for reports of laser light show injuries to the general public. To the best of our knowledge, there have been roughly five such injuries, worldwide, from continuous wave lasers since laser shows began about 50 years ago. This is about one per decade, worldwide.
This tally counts not only U.S. regulated laser light shows, but also overseas shows. The majority of these shows are not done to U.S.-type regulations. They usually involve directly scanning laser light onto the audience (which FDA prohibits unless a special “audience scanning variance” has been approved).
Even in the U.S., many or perhaps a majority of laser light shows are done illegally, with no FDA variance. Almost all laser light shows are never inspected by FDA. Both of these facts are of concern to ILDA.
From a safety standpoint, however, these facts indicate that even without much regulatory oversight, laser light show eye injuries from CW lasers are exceedingly unlikely — on the order of one per decade of laser light shows, worldwide.
In 2009, ILDA presented a 31-page paper at the International Laser Safety Conference regarding this topic in much more detail. The paper includes reasons why laser light is much more hazardous close to the laser (e.g, for technicians, persons in industry and medicine, etc.) than it is for an audience further away. The paper is online at ilda.com/resources/Safety/ audience-scanning_overview_latest.pdf. The first 17 pages are most relevant to this discussion. We ask them to be included in our comments by reference.
PULSED LASERS REQUIRE REGULATION
The above discussion of safety is true for continuous wave lasers. Unfortunately, there have been about 50 injuries from pulsed lasers over the same time period. The good news is that almost all laser shows today use only continuous wave lasers. We believe any potential issue
Page 5 of 14
with pulsed lasers in Texas can be handled under the current and proposed regulations. This will be discussed later.
NO REQUIREMENT FOR A REGULATORY BURDEN
ILDA assumes that Texas’ policy is to involve government when necessary, but also to reduce the regulatory burden on it’s citizens and businesses when this is justified. We also assume Texas is a “pro-business” state that wants to see its businesses succeed and not be tied up in unnecessary red tape.
ILDA’s recommendation therefore is that Texas drop both the requirement for registration of continuous wave light show lasers, and related requirements such as notification of Texas laser light shows.
Again, if there has been no harm to Texans (or other persons worldwide), why are light show lasers and their use regulated to such a degree?
FUNDING THE DEPARTMENT?
ILDA does agree that persons and companies being regulated should help pay for necessary regulations and oversight (e.g., inspectors, paperwork review, etc.). However, this assumes that the regulations are necessary.
If registration of light show lasers is being done primarily to help fund the Department of State Health Services, we believe this is wrong.
As stated above, ILDA does not see any assertions or evidence from DSHS of any injury to Texans from laser light shows.
PIGGYBACK OFF U.S. FDA VARIANCES
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration requires that a variance be obtained for any laser light show or display of 5 milliwatts or more which is entered into commerce. An FDA variance covers both the laser light show equipment and how it is used in a laser light show.
There are four relevant FDA Forms: 3632 for the laser projector, 3147 for the variance, 3640 for the laser light show itself, and 3636 for the annual report due every year. These reports (or links to the projector reports) are required by FDA to be onsite at every laser light show and display.
Thus, if Texas wants to review these, they are onsite at a laser light show. If they are not onsite, the show is illegal from a federal standpoint and Texas certainly would be within its rights to ban or stop the show.
Texas could also require that copies be emailed or postal mailed to, DSHS upon request, based on a reasonable rationale for the request.
Page 6 of 14
Having these documents onsite at a laser light show, or providing them upon request to DSHS, helps prove that the laser light show producer has followed FDA regulations. This turn implies they will be doing a safe and legal show.
If the forms are not available onsite, then the laser light show should not be permitted. Similarly, if copies of the requested forms are not emailed or sent via postal mail to DSHS, the person or company should not be permitted to perform laser light shows in Texas.
ADVANCE NOTIFICATION SHOULD NO LONGER BE REQUIRED
FDA has had so few problems with laser light shows that they no longer variance holders to provide, in advance, a list of laser light show tour itineraries. They can request an itinerary of any variance holder, but as far as ILDA knows, FDA does not often do this.
Since FDA does not require such notification, ILDA believes that advance notification of laser light shows should also not be required by Texas, especially in light of the lack of documented injuries from laser light shows in Texas.
IF ADVANCE NOTIFICATION CONTINUES TO BE REQUIRED
If Texas continues to require advance notification about laser light shows, at the very least the information requested should be scaled back substantially. Most of the information requested on the current BRC Form 301-C is already in the FDA documentation. There is no reason for Texas to require the applicant to repeat information already in the FDA-approved variance and other forms.
Now, if a person or company filling out the advance notification form does NOT have FDA documentation, then DSHS should be required to refuse permission for the show, and should take positive steps to ensure that the show not be performed.
This is not so much for safety reasons, since as stated above there appear to be no injuries from laser light show operations in Texas. The reason is for regulatory fairness. It is unfair and possibly illegal if Texas actively requires laser light show operators to have relevant FDA and FAA approvals, but then does not stop shows without such approvals.
ILDA would also ask that DSHS take positive steps to ensure that the show not be performed. This could include contacting the person who hired the laser show and/or the venue owner, to tell them the show cannot proceed unless proper FDA documentation is provided in time to DSHS. (This means the form should ask for the name and contact information of the person or company hiring the laser light show, and of the venue representative if known.) This could also include having the police or fire department, or other authority check the venue on the show date to ensure lasers are not being used, and are stopped if they are in use.
Page 7 of 14
Note that if DSHS decides not to require advance notification, as recommended by ILDA, then there would be no legal requirement for DSHS to actively monitor and stop shows that do not have FDA approval.
NO NEED FOR STATE REGISTRATION AGENT
ILDA is concerned about the requirement for a state registration agent, for show producers located outside of Texas.
We are unaware of any state radiological health agency that requires such registration. (New York State has a somewhat different system, run through their Department of Labor, not radiation safety.)
Requiring a state agent to file RC Form 301-05 (BRC Form 301-3) is unnecessary. Certainly if someone has obtained a valid laser light show variance from FDA, they are qualified at the federal level at least. FDA did not require them to go through an agent.
TEXAS ADOPTION OF FDA VARIANCES
FDA allows intentional scanning of audiences with laser light if the variance applicant is using special equipment and procedures. Such an “audience scanning variance” will include an attachment specifically addressing such equipment and procedures.
ILDA is concerned about language such as paragraph (c)(2) which prohibits individuals being intentionally exposed to laser and IPL radiation above the MPE.
This could be interpreted by an overzealous DSHS official as prohibiting any audience exposure, even FDA-approved audience scanning. As far as we know, FDA does not allow audience scanning above the MPE. But this distinction may be lost on an official with insufficient knowledge or training.
Therefore, ILDA suggests having language in the Texas regulations similar to this: “Laser light shows performed in Texas must be done in accordance with the requirements stated in the FDA variance and any other relevant FDA forms, documents and regulations.”
LASER SAFETY OFFICERS ARE SEPARATE FROM LASERS
ILDA and Board Member Roberta McHatton have trained 750 Laser Safety Officers specifically for doing laser light shows. These persons have passed tests in order to receive a Certificate of Completion. Both ILDA and Roberta are on the ANSI Z136.1 “Safe Use of Lasers” committee which developed and updates definitions including that of Laser Safety Officers.
We therefore have some expertise in the definition and requirements for a Laser Safety Officer.
Page 8 of 14
In the Definitions section, the first part of the definition of “Laser Safety Officer” is fine. (“An individual who has a knowledge of and the authority and responsibility to apply appropriate laser radiation protection rules, standards, and practices.”) This accords with ANSI and other states’ definitions.
However, ILDA strongly recommends that Texas’ definition of “Laser Safety Officer” NOT include the phrase of “and who must be specifically authorized on a certificate of laser registration.” This a Texas-specific requirement which would not apply in other states.
Furthermore, regardless of the definition wording, LSOs should not be coupled to laser registration certificates — at least not for laser light shows. This is for three reasons:
The lack of injuries from laser light shows means there should be little or no regulation beyond existing, sufficient federal regulations.
An LSO is not responsible under ANSI or other states for a specific laser. They are responsible for all lasers being used under their supervision — but not for a specific model or serial number laser.
This would be like requiring all drivers’ licenses to be specifically tied to a certificate of automobile registration. I would not be able to drive a car other than the one(s) registered in my name.
LSOs ARE DIFFERENT FROM LASER OPERATORS
ILDA is very concerned that Texas either does not understand a laser light show LSO’s responsibilities, or that the responsibilities are unclear in the regulations.
A laser light show Laser Safety Officer is responsible for the safety of the show, both in design and operation. However, the LSO does NOT have to be the person who operates the show.
This is a separate safety position, the Laser Operator. (ILDA uses the slightly different wording “Lasershow Operator” to make clear that this person is operating the show as well as the laser device).
On page 20 is a requirement that the LSO “assume control and have the authority to institute corrective actions to include the shutdown of operations when necessary in emergencies or unsafe conditions.” This incorrectly implies that the LSO is the one who is assuming control or is otherwise in direct control of operations.
A better wording would be something like the following:
“If the LSO is not in direct control of the laser operation, they shall be responsible for the training and actions of a Laser Operator. The Laser Operator shall be trained on the operation of the laser, laser system, and associated laser safety devices and features. The training shall cover both normal operation, and what to do in case of abnormal operation,
Page 9 of 14
misalignment, malfunctions, and other reasonably foreseeable potential hazards. The Laser Operator shall follow all safety instructions of the Laser Safety Officer.”
That is what actually happens at most laser light shows. It is not necessary for the Laser Operator to have all of the knowledge that an LSO must have.
In many cases, the Laser Operator’s duties are basic: To activate the laser at the appropriate time, to monitor the operation, and — if safety hazards arise or are imminent — to shut off the laser or take other corrective action.
LASER LIGHT SHOWS NEEDING REGULATION
As stated earlier, the lack of injuries from laser light shows implies that laser registration and advance notification is not needed at this time.
There may be two special types of lasers where registration and notification are justified:
Lasers that operate, or could be operated, in pulsed mode. This means the laser light is emitted in pulses rather than continuously. This could be a single pulse, or more commonly in laser light shows, in repetitive pulses. Note that “pulsed laser” does NOT include lasers whose light is emitted continuously (“continuous wave” or “CW”) and is then modulated or chopped to create a pulsed effect.
The reason for this is that pulsed lasers in general have a much higher peak power in each pulse. Pulsed lasers misused have caused injuries at a number of laser shows in the past 50 years.
The worst documented laser light show injury incident was in July 2008 when a pulsed laser was aimed at an audience during a rave in Russia. Thirty-five persons went to the hospital with eye injuries or complaints. Four were found to have permanent injuries (though no permanent blindness as far as ILDA knows).
In our Laser Safety Officer classes, both ILDA and Roberta McHatton stress that pulsed lasers should never be used on or near any person. Fortunately, almost all laser shows today use CW lasers exclusively.
Lasers that are over a certain average power, whether pulsed or CW. ILDA’s preliminary suggestion is that Texas register laser light show lasers emitting one or more beams with 50 or more watts average power. Note that this power level is preliminary, because ILDA has not yet consulted with other laser light show safety experts, and those doing laser light shows, to see if they concur with this particular wattage.
It is also important to note the difference between “50 watts per beam” and a 50 watt laser. Some laser projection devices use a diffraction grating to break up a single beam into multiple, simultaneous “beamlets”. Each beamlet will have a fraction of the original laser
Page 10 of 14
power. So any power limit should be per beam (e.g., the highest power of any beam possible from the laser projector), not the power of the laser inside the projector.
This is NOT the case for scanned beams, where a single beam is rapidly moved by tiny galvanometer mirrors under computer control. Although the average power of the beam is reduced by scanning, it is possible for scanning to stop due to mechanical failure (a broken scanner) or, more likely, due to improper software control. Laser show projectors using scanners should be evaluated for the maximum output power of the laser, regardless of whether the beam is scanned or is stationary.
In general, if Texas wants to register laser light show lasers, this should only be done for hazardous lasers such as pulsed lasers, or those with powers that are significantly above the powers that have been used for the past 50 years.
Thus far ILDA has not seen injuries from higher-powered lasers. But since safe and effective shows have been done for 50 years with powers less than 50 watts per beam, ILDA does not see any demonstrated need for registering light show lasers less than 50 watts (or whatever wattage is recommended as “extra powerful” by laser light show safety experts and those doing shows).
COST OF REGISTRATION
It is not clear from RC 204 (Fee Schedule) if the registration cost for “Laser - Industrial/ Services/Entertainment” is $400 per laser or $400 per registering all lasers being used. (Hopefully it is $400 per person or company that is registering their lasers.)
Either way, ILDA believes that registration should not be required for CW laser light show lasers under a power limit (provisionally 50 watt output) due to the demonstrated lack of injuries both in Texas and worldwide.
There may be justification for registering pulsed lasers used in entertainment, or lasers emitting one or more beams over a certain wattage.
REPEATING INFORMATION IN OTHER DOCUMENTS
In the regulation, there is a large amount of information that repeats what is in ANSI Z136 “Safe Use of Lasers”, IEC 60825-1 “Safety of laser products”, and U.S. FDA regulations.
The repeated material includes definitions, practices and signage requirements.
It would be very helpful to specifically state both when Texas’ regulations are the same as these sources, and when the regs differ.
Page 11 of 14
For those who are already familiar with ANSI, IEC and FDA, this lets us “skip over” parts that are the same. More importantly, it highlights or warns us of any areas where Texas’ requirements differ from ANSI, IEC and FDA.
SPECIFIC LANGUAGE
Here is a list of the most important sections we recommend to be removed or changed:
(d)(33), definition of Laser Safety Officer: Remove the second phrase “and who must be specifically authorized on a certificate of laser registration.”
(f)(1)(E): We believe registration of laser light show lasers should not be required, due to the lack of demonstrated injuries caused by such lasers.
(g)(1)(C) and subsections (i) and (ii): If registration of light show lasers is deemed to be required, we believe such registration of laser light show lasers should not need to be accompanied by a Business Information Form or the other requirements in (i) and (ii). It should be sufficient for the applicant to list their business name on the application form, and if necessary, to also list their business registration number as issued by Texas or their state (or province for non-U.S. businesses). ILDA does not know why Texas goes to lengths that even FDA does not to “prove” that a business is actually a business.
(g)(5) Application for laser light show: As stated earlier, we do not see any demonstrated harm from laser light shows nor any demonstrated necessity to register shows (as opposed to registering light show laser devices). We therefore recommend that section (g)(5) be rewritten using language such as this:
(g)(5) “Laser light shows
(A) Any laser light shows using pulsed lasers, or using high-power lasers (where one or more emitted beams is over 50 watts), shall apply for and receive a certificate of laser registration for a pulsed or high-power laser light show before beginning any such show.
Any application for a pulsed or high-power laser light show must be signed by the applicant, registrant, or a person duly authorized to act for and on behalf of the applicant or registrant. The application must also be signed by the LSO, if the applicant or registrant is not a Laser Safety Officer.
All pulsed lasers, and lasers where one or more emitted beams is over 50 watts shall be registered with the state.
(B) All laser light shows performed in the state of Texas shall have a valid variance issued from the FDA, along with evidence of annual report(s) keeping the variance valid, and all other documents required by FDA. For shows where laser light enters or traverses navigable airspace (including potential navigation by helicopters and emergency aircraft),
Page 12 of 14
the show shall also have a valid Letter of Determination from the Federal Aviation Administration, not objecting to the show and its effects.
All documentation required by FDA (and, if appropriate, FAA) shall be available onsite for inspection upon demand.
The department may contact the laser light show LSO, producer, or other official to obtain a copy of any FDA or FAA document required by FDA to be onsite during a laser light show. There shall be stated reasons or justification for the demand.
If any laser light show performed or planned to be performed in the state of Texas does not have all valid documents required by FDA (including, if appropriate, an FAA Letter of Determination not objecting to the show), then the show cannot proceed.”
This also implies a new definition in section (d):
(d)(23) High-powered laser—For purposes of these regulations, a laser where one or more of the emitted beams has a power greater than 50 watts. If diffraction gratings or other elements are used to modify a laser, normally emitting over 50 watts, so no single beam is over 50 watts, then the grating or other element shall be fixed such that it cannot be removed during operation without the use of tools.
Continuing the list of recommended changes:
(p)(2)(A) [new subsection]: “If the LSO is not in direct control of the laser operation, they shall be responsible for the training and actions of a Laser Operator. The Laser Operator shall be trained on the operation of the laser, laser system, and associated laser safety devices and features. The training shall cover both normal operation, and what to do in case of abnormal operation, misalignment, malfunctions, and other reasonably foreseeable potential hazards. The Laser Operator shall follow all safety instructions of the Laser Safety Officer.”
(q)(1)(A) [new subsection]: “For laser light shows, exposure of any individual to laser light on their body (including on clothing) shall not be permitted except as authorized in a valid FDA variance which specifically allows such exposure.”
(q)(3)(A) through (q)(4) — Much of this parallels requirements already in 21 CFR 1040.10 and/or 1040.11, and other FDA documents such as a laser product report and variances. The Texas requirements should be reviewed to remove any duplicated requirements.
(s)(1) — This section does not define “access” to IPL, Class 3B or Class 4 levels of laser radiation. Often in laser light shows a person is mounting a projector while it is off (no access) and then is far from the laser while aligning, rehearsing and performing the laser light show. In such cases, protective eyewear is not needed, just as it is not needed for the audience. (In fact, it would be counterproductive for an audience to wear protective eyewear as they cannot see the laser effects.) This provision should be clarified for laser light show personnel.
Page 13 of 14
(u)(3)(D) through (u)(3)(G) — We believe that FDA allows use of the term “light” instead of “laser radiation” or “electromagnetic radiation” or similar, for laser radiation in the 400 -700 nanometer wavelength range. Therefore we recommend a new subsection:
(u)(3)(G)(iii): “The word ‘light’ may be used in place of ‘laser radiation’ or ‘electromagnetic radiation’ on labels and signs required by this subparagraph for wavelengths that are exclusively in the range of 400 to 700 nm.”
(u)(4): This language should be moved higher in the (u) section, perhaps even to the start. The reason is that the knowledgable reader will not have to wade through all of section (u) to find out that their existing FDA, ANSI or IEC labels and signage are acceptable by the state of Texas. They should be told this “upfront” so they can skim or skip the rest of section (u). (If there are any Texas-specific labels or signage, not required by FDA, ANSI or IEC, these should be called out for the knowledgeable reader.)
(x): The phrase “calibrated and designed” should be clarified and strengthened to “calibrated, designed, and appropriate”. Why? To give an example, silicon power meters are appropriate for measuring moving beams; they can give an instant indication of the power level. However, thermal meters — which can measure static beams without a problem — are not appropriate for measuring moving beams. This clarifies that the meter must be appropriate for the use of the laser being tested.
SUMMARY
ILDA greatly appreciates that Texas is concerned with laser safety in general, and with laser light shows in particular.
However, the association believes there is some overregulation for laser light shows. ILDA hopes that Texas will reexamine their requirements with a fresh eye.
Sincerely,
Patrick Murphy
